title: 📖 מחזור לחג השבועות (אשכנז) | Maḥzor l'Ḥag ha-Shavuot, a prayerbook for the Festival of Weeks as arranged by Wolf Heidenheim (translation by Jenny Marmorstein 1967)
author: Aharon N. Varady (digital imaging and document preparation)

coauthors.name: Aharon N. Varady (digital imaging and document preparation)
coauthors.bio: Aharon Varady, founding director of the Open Siddur Project, is a copyright researcher and amateur book scanner. He prepares digital images and new digital editions of prayer books and related works in the Public Domain in order to make their constituent parts (prayers, translations, annotations, etc.) publicly accessible for collaborative transcription by project volunteers. (In some cases, he finds existing digital editions prepared by others that require correction and reformatting.) If you appreciate his efforts, please send him a kind note or contribute to his patreon account.

coauthors.name: Jenny Marmorstein (translation)
coauthors.bio: Jenny (Rivqah) Marmorstein née Blau (1920-2020), born in Hamburg, Germany, was a translator of German and Hebrew. The daughter of two Jewish educators, she grew up in the predominantly Jewish Grindelviertel neighborhood attending the Israelitische Töchterschule on Karolinenstraße. After graduating she attended a two-year gardening course in 1936 at the Hachshara Home on Wilhelminenhöhe, a training center for young people who wanted to emigrate to Palestine. Her planned emigration could not be realized due to the limited immigration quotas for British Mandate Palestine, and in April 1939, her family managed to find her passage to London with a domestic permit—a residence permit for domestic workers—where an aunt and uncle of hers were already living. After World War Ⅱ, she studied Hebrew and Arabic at the School of Oriental Studies in London. In the 1960s, her translation of Wolf Heidenheim's arrangement of the festival maḥzorim was published by Victor Goldschmidt Publishers Basle. Together with her husband Rabbi Emile Marmorestein, she published Piyyutim: liturgical poems for the year's special Sabbath (Feldheim: 1983). In the 1990s, she completed a university degree in history and literature.

coauthors.name: Wolf Heidenheim
coauthors.bio: Benjamin Wolf ben Samson Heidenheim (בנימין זאב בן שמשון היידנהיים;; 1757–1832), born in Heidenheim, Germany, was a German-Jewish exegete and grammarian. At an early age Heidenheim was sent to Fürth, where he studied Talmud under Joseph Steinhardt (author of Zikron Yosef) and, from 1777, under Hirsch Janow. Besides Talmudic literature, Heidenheim devoted himself to the study of Hebrew grammar, and particularly that of the Masorah. In 1782 he left Fürth, probably on account of Janow's opposition to Mendelssohn's translation of the Pentateuch, of which Heidenheim was an admirer. He went to Frankfurt, where he made the acquaintance of the most prominent scholars, among them Wolf Breidenbach and Solomon Dubno. There began his literary activity, which lasted fifty years. In addition to his work on Hebrew grammar and critical texts of the Masoretic bible, Heidenheim produced a still-valued edition of the maḥzor based on existing medieval manuscripts.

excerpt: מחזור לחג השבועות Prayerbook for the Feast of Weeks is volume 7 in a set of maḥzorim reprinting Wolf Heidenheim's arrangement of liturgy according to the Ashkenazi custom, accompanied by an English translation by Jenny Marmorstein, z"l (1920-2020).
content:
מחזור לחג השבועות Prayerbook for the Feast of Weeks is volume 7 in a set of maḥzorim reprinting Wolf Heidenheim’s arrangement of liturgy according to the Ashkenazi custom, accompanied by an English translation by Jenny Marmorstein, z”l (1920-2020).

This work is in the Public Domain according to the 1994 Uruguay Round Agreements Act due to the expiration of the term of copyright in its home country (50 years after publication in Switzerland).
